summary: Atlanta City Council members are raising red flags about how the city will handle homelessness during the World Cup, with a new state bill and ongoing encampment sweeps adding pressure. Urbanize Atlanta got a look at the second phase of the Halidom development rising on Moreland just south of East Atlanta Village. And the Atlanta Influences Everything brand planted a flag on Concourse B with a new airport store, expanding from its Edgewood Avenue hub.

topStory.headline: Pre-World Cup, Atlanta Council Members Ask How the City Will Police Homelessness
topStory.summary: Councilmember Jason Dozier warned Tuesday that a state bill pressuring cities to enforce quality-of-life laws against unhoused residents could force Atlanta into a crackdown during the FIFA tournament. The council committee also pressed staff on encampment-clearance protocols reformed after the 2024 death of Cornelius Taylor.
topStory.body:
Several Atlanta City Council members raised fears Tuesday that the 2026 FIFA World Cup could mean a law enforcement crackdown on homelessness, with police displacing or even arresting unsheltered residents near downtown matches.

"There's a concern that we are at risk of criminalizing homelessness in a way that is contrary to the values that we have as a city," District 4 Councilmember Jason Dozier said during an April 21 work session held by the council's Community Development and Human Services Committee on the city's World Cup preparations.

Dozier said House Bill 295, awaiting Gov. Brian Kemp's signature, will complicate Atlanta's ability to compassionately address homelessness before, during, and after the tournament. The bill pressures local governments to enforce quality-of-life laws against unhoused people by letting property owners sue if cities don't. That includes offenses like public camping, loitering, panhandling, shoplifting, and public drug use. "Our state leadership has not been as values-driven as we have been as a city," Dozier said. "That [bill] puts legal risk on municipalities if they don't clean up or criminalize homelessness in these sorts of ways."

The committee also reviewed the city's reformed encampment-clearance protocols, which were overhauled last year after a tent-city sweep left 47-year-old Cornelius Taylor dead, crushed by a Department of Public Works bulldozer. The new protocols mandate advance warnings and greater coordination with outreach providers before any clearance.

Eight World Cup matches will be played at Mercedes-Benz Stadium between June 13 and July 15. Monday night's council vote created a public entertainment district spanning downtown for the duration of the tournament, stripping out the open container provision over concerns from Castleberry Hill and downtown residents.
topStory.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council committee discussing World Cup preparations

stories.headline: Halidom's Phase Two Rises on Moreland Just South of East Atlanta Village
stories.summary: A two-story, 18,000-square-foot retail building is going up next to the existing Halidom Eatery food hall. Developer IST Management Services says leases are signed for tenants mixing retail and light medical, with announcements coming soon.
stories.body:
Atlanta's South Beach-style food hall is getting new neighbors. New construction rising in the 1300 block of Moreland Avenue, south of East Atlanta Village, is the second phase of the Halidom mixed-use development that started opening with offices back in 2022.

Halidom and its 11-stall food hall component, Halidom Eatery, sit in the Woodland Hills pocket between East Atlanta Village and the Starlight Drive In, an area that has seen a steady drip of post-pandemic residential investment.

Under construction now is a two-story retail building bringing a mix of retail and light medical to Southeast Atlanta, spanning just under 18,000 square feet, according to Brad Burgess, vice president of marketing and real estate for developer IST Management Services, the property's main tenant. "We've already secured a couple of leases that we're not quite ready to announce yet, but they're the type of additions we believe the community will be genuinely excited about," Burgess wrote.

The new building sits between Halidom Eatery and a versatile park space the project has maintained for outdoor events. For the EAV-adjacent corridor, Halidom continues to be one of the more interesting bets on Southeast Atlanta as a standalone food-and-retail destination, rather than just a commute stop between neighborhoods.
stories.imageAlt: Halidom phase two construction on Moreland Avenue

stories.headline: Wayfair Opens 150,000-Square-Foot Atlanta Superstore at The District at Howell Mill
stories.summary: The online home retailer's second U.S. brick-and-mortar carries furniture, decor, housewares, bedding, bath, and home improvement, alongside a cafe and in-store design consults. Wayfair says the location was picked to serve the broader Southeast, not just Atlanta.
stories.body:
Wayfair has opened a 150,000-square-foot superstore at The District at Howell Mill, its second brick-and-mortar store in the country. The first is in Wilmette, Illinois, just outside Chicago.

"Atlanta is one of the most dynamic home markets in the country," Vice President of Merchandising and Stores Liza Lefkowski said. "It's filled with creativity, growth, and people who want to invest in their homes. We already have a strong connection with customers here, and we look forward to building on that momentum."

The store carries merchandise for every room, including furniture, decor, housewares, bedding and bath, home improvement items, and seasonal goods. Lefkowski said Atlanta gave the company a chance to serve the broader Southeast with "a space that sparks ideas and makes home projects feel approachable."

Shoppers will find a cafe, in-store design advisors, and displays meant to help customers picture pieces in real rooms rather than scroll through listings. For The District at Howell Mill, Wayfair's footprint joins a mix that already leans on big-box anchors, and adds another reason for West Midtown home shoppers to stay on the west side of the Connector.
stories.imageAlt: Wayfair superstore at The District at Howell Mill

stories.headline: Jagger Suite Rooftop Atop Moxy Marietta Street Leans Into Disco-Era Cocktails
stories.summary: The new lounge on top of the Moxy Hotel at 329 Marietta St. NW added a golden-hour happy hour menu with elevated bites. A 12-foot horse sculpture, retro furnishings, and skyline views anchor the mood.
stories.body:
Atlanta's newest rooftop cocktail lounge, Jagger Suite, is settling into its perch atop the Moxy Hotel at 329 Marietta St. NW with an unapologetically '70s sensibility. A 12-foot horse sculpture, retro furnishings, and a disco-leaning cocktail list make the space feel more like a set piece than a hotel bar.

The lounge celebrated its grand opening in early March. It recently rolled out a Golden Hour (happy hour) menu of small bites and drinks served as the sun sets behind the skyline. Think lighter plates designed to pair with the kind of cocktail you'd want to hold in a photo.

Jagger Suite joins a run of new downtown openings that are trying to keep the neighborhood interesting past the convention-center crowd. It also lands a short walk from the Mercedes-Benz Stadium and State Farm Arena footprint, putting it in range of the World Cup entertainment district the city just carved out for June and July.
stories.imageAlt: Jagger Suite rooftop lounge atop the Moxy Hotel in downtown Atlanta

stories.headline: Silverspoon Cafe Heading to Ellis Street Downtown This Spring
stories.summary: Owner Jacquelyne Roberson plans a Monday-Friday 7 a.m. to 4 p.m. cafe at 151 Ellis St. NE, according to a business license filing. She also runs Silverspoon Catering, and hopes to open by May pending city permits.
stories.body:
A new cafe is heading downtown this spring. Silverspoon Cafe will open at 151 Ellis St. NE, according to a recent business license filing with the City of Atlanta.

Owner Jacquelyne Roberson, who also runs Silverspoon Catering, told What Now Atlanta she plans to operate the cafe Monday through Friday from 7 a.m. to 4 p.m. She is aiming for a May opening, though the final timing hinges on city permitting.

Menu and design details are still under wraps. For downtown, a weekday-breakfast-and-lunch spot on Ellis is the kind of independent opening the area has been quietly collecting as more residents move into the surrounding towers and the World Cup push brings new foot traffic through the core.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of Silverspoon Cafe coming to Ellis Street downtown

stories.headline: Atlanta Influences Everything Opens Concourse B Store at Hartsfield-Jackson
stories.summary: The brand founded by Bem Joiner, Tory Edwards, and Ian Ford grew out of the AIE hub on Edgewood Avenue. Mayor Andre Dickens was scheduled to attend the airport opening, which sells exclusive apparel and pieces from local creators.
stories.body:
The Atlanta Influences Everything brand, born from a phrase Atlanta native Bem Joiner made into a movement, now has a storefront inside the busiest airport in the world. The latest AIE outpost opened Wednesday on Concourse B at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, with Mayor Andre Dickens among the notable guests expected.

AIE is the company founded by Tory Edwards (operations and content), Joiner (innovation and civic), and Ian Ford (creative). It has spun the "Atlanta Influences Everything" mantra into 404 Day, Atlanta United uniforms, local and international collaborations, and a growing line of apparel.

"This is big, not only for us but for the city we love," Ford told Capital B Atlanta. "We know and believe that Atlanta really does influence everything. And now we are on a huge stage to share this belief with the world. This is the busiest airport in the world, with millions of people touching down every week."

The store stocks a curated run of exclusive apparel and items from local creators. It expands from AIE's flagship hub on Edgewood Avenue, which remains the cultural anchor for the brand. For the city's Edgewood corridor, the airport win is a nice signal boost for one of Atlanta's most defining independent labels.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta Influences Everything storefront at Hartsfield-Jackson

stories.headline: Atlanta Press Club's Largest-Ever Debate Series Runs April 26 to 28
stories.summary: The 30-year-old Loudermilk-Young series will stage 18 primary debates over three days with nearly 85 candidates, including governor, U.S. Senate, attorney general, and Public Service Commission. All segments air on Georgia Public Broadcasting and YouTube.
stories.body:
The Atlanta Press Club is about to stage its biggest debate year ever. From April 26 to 28, the 30-year-old Loudermilk-Young debate series will bring nearly 85 candidates to the stage for 18 different primary election debates.

"We're like a little factory over three days," Atlanta Press Club past president and debate logistics coordinator Lauri Strauss said. Each of the 18 debates will air in short segments on Georgia Public Broadcasting or YouTube, where they stay available on demand.

The format stays fast: local journalists act as panelists, each candidate has 60 seconds to answer, and 30 seconds for rebuttal. "We really try to make the debates what is most informative for voters," Strauss said. This year's lineup pulls in candidates for U.S. Senate, Governor, Secretary of State, Lt. Governor, Attorney General, Public Service Commissioner, and U.S. Congressional Districts 1, 10, 11, and 13.

Grant support from the Arthur M. Blank Family Foundation and the Robert Charles Loudermilk Sr. Foundation is keeping the televised series going. Early voting for the primary starts April 27, and primary day is May 19.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta Press Club Loudermilk-Young debate series logo

stories.headline: Seven Gubernatorial Candidates Face Off on Affordability and Education at Leadership DeKalb Forum
stories.summary: The April 21 forum at Porter Sanford III Performing Arts and Community Center drew around 200 attendees in person plus 350 watching online. Participants included Olu Brown, Geoff Duncan, Jason Esteves, Derrick Jackson, Michael Thurmond, Clark Dean, and Kenneth Yasger.
stories.body:
Seven of the 14 candidates running for governor took the stage April 21 at Leadership DeKalb's primary forum to answer questions on affordability, education funding, and leadership. The event drew roughly 200 in-person attendees at the Porter Sanford III Performing Arts and Community Center in South DeKalb, plus 350 watching via livestream.

Moderator Maya Prabhu, a political journalist and Spelman College alum, walked candidates through questions on their personal leadership experience, the policies they would champion as governor, and how they would respond to the rising cost of living in Georgia. Six Democrats participated: Impact Church Lead Pastor Olu Brown, former Lt. Governor Geoff Duncan, former State Senator Jason Esteves, State Rep. Derrick Jackson, and former DeKalb County CEO Michael Thurmond. Republican newcomers Clark Dean and Kenneth Yasger represented the GOP primary field.

All 14 candidates had been invited. The forum was the latest in a run of regional events (Esteves's Decatur town hall on data centers among them) staking out positions before the May 19 primary. Early voting starts April 27.

For Decatur-area voters, Leadership DeKalb's forum was a chance to see the field side-by-side on the affordability-and-education bundle that has shaped the primary so far.
stories.imageAlt: Gubernatorial candidates on stage at Porter Sanford III Performing Arts Center
